note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-14-01-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM, numpy rfft): the Σ(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 230.193s but the ffprobed master is 229.721s (0.472s off, past the ±0.3s gate). Cross-correlation shows the master is the 13 blocks concatenated with a 0.4s gap after each; per-file ffprobe durations are inflated ~0.036s each by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, and the error accumulates (diff reaches -0.472s by s13), which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset (they land exactly on Σ(PCM audio+0.4)); final end = ffprobed master duration 229.721s.

phrases.text: WashCentral ships with its own training platform, the Academy.
Click the graduation cap.
You land on My Dashboard, your learning home base.
Notice the top-right corner: search and Ask Shyne follow you in here too.
Same platform, different wing.
Read the sidebar before anything else.
The Academy group holds the four places a learner lives:
your dashboard, the catalog, learning paths, and your own course list.
Below it sits an Admin group.
That is the back office, another lesson entirely, and we stay out of it today.
Four tiles across the top.
Enrolled, completed, hours, certificates.
Every one reads zero because this account has never taken a course,
and that is worth seeing once:
it is exactly what a brand new client learner sees on day one.
These four numbers are your status at a glance.
Under the tiles, Continue Learning.
This is the resume slot.
When you are partway through a course, it surfaces here
so you pick up mid-lesson instead of hunting for where you left off.
On a fresh account it previews the starter course instead,
and View All opens the fuller list.
Look closer at the card, hands off the buttons.
Two chips set the category and the level, then the title,
a progress bar at zero of six lessons, and the total runtime, forty five minutes.
Start Course would enroll you, and this walk is browse only,
so it stays unclicked.
Below that, All Courses.
Each card follows one anatomy:
a category ribbon, the title, who teaches it and how long it runs,
a progress bar, and a status line.
Not started means available; a moving bar means enrolled.
Browse Catalog up in the corner reaches the full library.
Six courses on this dashboard.
They start with the platform basics at forty five minutes
and climb to a two hour fifteen minute analytics course.
Real instructors, real lesson counts.
Every one reads Not started here, and every Start button stays untouched.
Now My Achievements.
Three badges earned, three locked.
The earned ones render in full color;
the locked ones sit dimmed until you meet their condition.
A small thing, but it is the Academy's gamification layer,
and client crews notice it more than you would expect.
On the right, the Org Leaderboard.
Colleagues ranked by learning points,
with your own row highlighted so you can find yourself in the standings.
It is view only from here,
and it is the panel that turns a training rollout into a bit of a competition.
Under the standings, Quick Links.
A couple of shortcuts, your learning paths and your certificates,
that jump straight to those views without a trip through the sidebar.
Nothing deep here.
Just the fast lane.
Back to the sidebar for the other three stops.
The catalog is the full library, nineteen courses deep.
Learning Paths chains them into named tracks,
twenty two courses in all, none started on this account.
And My Learning keeps your own record across four tabs, every one at zero today.
We point without opening.
Their interiors are a walk for another lesson.
One rule before you explore on your own.
Browsing, hovering, reading: all free.
Enrolling is not.
Start Course, Start, and any enroll button create real training records,
so in this lesson none of them get clicked.
Taking a course comes later.
That is the learner side.
Four tiles for your status, Continue Learning to resume,
the course grid and catalog to browse,
badges and the leaderboard to keep score,
and three more sidebar stops when you are ready.
You read all of it without enrolling in a thing.
